r convert month year to ordered numeric  I have a dataset with column where the values are month year in format like this ```  M_Yr  March 1990  April 1990  May   1990  June  1990  July  1990  Aug   1990  Sept  1990  Oct   1990  Nov   1990  Dec   1990  Jan   1991  Feb   1991  March 1991  April 1991  May   1991  June  1991  July  1991  Aug   1991  Sept  1991  Oct   1991  Nov   1991  Dec   1991 ```  I tried this approach. ``` df$Col1 <- as.numeric(df$M_Yr) ```  This does converts the `month Year` variable to numeric but the order is scrambled and not in right sequence. So I am wondering what is an efficient way to create this numeric variable without writing a lengthy `case_when` statement.  Any suggestion is much appreciated. useState set method not changing my state - react native  I'm working on a part of an application dealing with a deck of cards. For however many cards a player is supposed to have, I am looping through a deck of cards held in a state, randomly picking a card, assigning it to that player and then removing it from the deck. This all works correctly but when the deck is empty (.length === 0), I want to invoke a shuffle() method that will set the deck equal to the discard pile which is also an array held in a useState. The discard pile is also working as expected but for some reason when trying to set the currentDeck to the discard pile, using setCurrentDeck(discardPile), the currentDeck array remains empty. (I've also tried setCurrentDeck([...discardPile])).  I know this mostly likely has to do with how React handles batching hook calls, and that the value of currentDeck is not what I think it is because of the other setCurrentDeck() calls, but I'm still not able to figure out what's going on.  My code (simplified): ``` const [currentDeck, setCurrentDeck] = useState(deck) // 'deck' is a hard coded array of cards const [discardPile, setDiscardPile] = useState([]) const [faceUpCard, setFaceUpCard] = useState(null)    useEffect(() => {     if (currentDeck.length === 0) {       const temp = discardPile;       setCurrentDeck([...temp]);       setDiscardPile([]);     }   }, [currentDeck]);    function deal() {     if (readyToDeal) {       for (let i = 0; i < round + 2; i++) {         for (let j = 0; j < players.length; j++) {           const rand = Math.floor(Math.random() * currentDeck.length);           // players are assigned cards           currentDeck.splice(rand, 1); // card that was assigned is removed from deck           setCurrentDeck([...currentDeck]);         }       }       const rand = Math.floor(Math.random() * currentDeck.length);       setFaceUpCard(currentDeck[rand]);       currentDeck.splice(rand, 1);       setCurrentDeck([...currentDeck]);       setReadyToDeal(false);     }   } ```  I have also tried setting currentDeck with a shuffle method like this: ```   function shuffle() {     const temp = discardPile;     setCurrentDeck([ ...temp]);     setDiscardPile([]);   }    function deal() {     if (readyToDeal) {       for (let i = 0; i < round + 2; i++) {         for (let j = 0; j < players.length; j++) {           if (currentDeck.length === 0) {             shuffle();           }           const rand = Math.floor(Math.random() * currentDeck.length);           // players are assigned cards           currentDeck.splice(rand, 1); // card that was assigned is removed from deck           setCurrentDeck([...currentDeck]);         }       }       const rand = Math.floor(Math.random() * currentDeck.length);       setFaceUpCard(currentDeck[rand]);       currentDeck.splice(rand, 1);       setCurrentDeck([...currentDeck]);       setReadyToDeal(false);     }   } ```  but still no luck. Any help or advice would be appreciated. Thank you! useState set method not changing my state - react native  I'm working on a part of an application dealing with a deck of cards. For however many cards a player is supposed to have, I am looping through a deck of cards held in a state, randomly picking a card, assigning it to that player and then removing it from the deck. This all works correctly but when the deck is empty (.length === 0), I want to invoke a shuffle() method that will set the deck equal to the discard pile which is also an array held in a useState. The discard pile is also working as expected but for some reason when trying to set the currentDeck to the discard pile, using setCurrentDeck(discardPile), the currentDeck array remains empty. (I've also tried setCurrentDeck([...discardPile])).  I know this mostly likely has to do with how React handles batching hook calls, and that the value of currentDeck is not what I think it is because of the other setCurrentDeck() calls, but I'm still not able to figure out what's going on.  My code (simplified): ``` const [currentDeck, setCurrentDeck] = useState(deck) // 'deck' is a hard coded array of cards const [discardPile, setDiscardPile] = useState([]) const [faceUpCard, setFaceUpCard] = useState(null)    useEffect(() => {     if (currentDeck.length === 0) {       const temp = discardPile;       setCurrentDeck([...temp]);       setDiscardPile([]);     }   }, [currentDeck]);    function deal() {     if (readyToDeal) {       for (let i = 0; i < round + 2; i++) {         for (let j = 0; j < players.length; j++) {           const rand = Math.floor(Math.random() * currentDeck.length);           // players are assigned cards           currentDeck.splice(rand, 1); // card that was assigned is removed from deck           setCurrentDeck([...currentDeck]);         }       }       const rand = Math.floor(Math.random() * currentDeck.length);       setFaceUpCard(currentDeck[rand]);       currentDeck.splice(rand, 1);       setCurrentDeck([...currentDeck]);       setReadyToDeal(false);     }   } ```  I have also tried setting currentDeck with a shuffle method like this: ```   function shuffle() {     const temp = discardPile;     setCurrentDeck([ ...temp]);     setDiscardPile([]);   }    function deal() {     if (readyToDeal) {       for (let i = 0; i < round + 2; i++) {         for (let j = 0; j < players.length; j++) {           if (currentDeck.length === 0) {             shuffle();           }           const rand = Math.floor(Math.random() * currentDeck.length);           // players are assigned cards           currentDeck.splice(rand, 1); // card that was assigned is removed from deck           setCurrentDeck([...currentDeck]);         }       }       const rand = Math.floor(Math.random() * currentDeck.length);       setFaceUpCard(currentDeck[rand]);       currentDeck.splice(rand, 1);       setCurrentDeck([...currentDeck]);       setReadyToDeal(false);     }   } ```  but still no luck. Any help or advice would be appreciated. Thank you! In React and React Native, calling setState (or, with hooks, the setter function from useState) does not immediately update the state value because state updates are asynchronous and may be batched. This means that when you call setCurrentDeck([...discardPile]) and immediately check currentDeck, it will not reflect the new value yet. If you need to perform actions immediately after your state has updated, consider using the useEffect hook to respond to changes in currentDeck. Additionally, avoid mutating the state array directly with methods like splice on currentDeck; instead, create new arrays when updating state to

Using discrete colors for map fill in mapboxgl instead of default interpoolated colors?  The way I've typically seen mapboxgl fill properties work on choropleth maps is something like this: ``` map.on('load', function () {       map.addSource('bb', { type: 'geojson', data: data, generateId: true});       map.addLayer({         'id': 'berlin',         'type': 'fill',         'source': 'bb',         'paint': {           'fill-color': {           'property': some_numeric_val,           'stops': [[4, '#feebe2'], [8, '#fbb4b9'], [12, '#f768a1'], [16, '#c51b8a'], [20, '#7a0177']]           },         'fill-opacity': .65           }       });       map.addLayer({         'id': 'berlin-stroke',         'type': 'line',         'source': 'bb',         'paint': {           'line-color': '#000',           'line-width': [           'case',             ['boolean', ['feature-state', 'hover'], false],             2,             .5           ]         }       });     }); ```  i.e. the colors are created based on a property that the user selects. However, it seems like mapboxgl's default behavior is to interpolate colors. For example, if one of my geographic units has a value is somewhere between the breakpoints, mapboxgl will interpolate the color, resulting in a gradient of colors.  Is there a way to make the colors distinct (non-interpolated)? i.e. if value is 4 or less, the color is #feebe2, if the value is 8 or less, the color is '#fbb4b9', for a total of 5 discrete colors in the example I have here.  I have not been able to find an answer to this anywhere. Thanks. Using discrete colors for map fill in mapboxgl instead of default interpoolated colors? Python requests module with multithread  Currently, I am developing the astrophotometric software for multiple telescopes. For this, I constructed a mother computer connected with multiple telescopes and these telescopes can be controlled by HTTP protocol. For synchronized operation, I am trying to control these multiple telescopes simultaneously with multithreading. However, when I retrieve image data (110MB for each, ~1.2GB for total) from multiple telescopes (10 telescopes), the data transfer speed is much slower than I expected. For seamless operation, we have 10G connection with the mother computer, and 1G connection with 10 telescopes. I expected ~9Gbps data transfer speed when 10 telescopes transfer the data simultaneously, but only 1.5Gbps achieved.  For simple test, I extracted the part of the code and check the time consumption. ``` import requests import time from astropy.time import Time def request_imagearray(cam):     client_trans_id = 1     client_id = 1     attribute = 'imagearray'      url = f"{cam.device.base_url}/{attribute}"     hdrs = {'accept' : 'application/imagebytes'}     # Make Host: header safe for IPv6     if(cam.device.address.startswith('[') and cam.device.address.startswith('[::1]')):         hdrs['Host'] = f'{cam.device.address.split("%")[0]}]'     pdata = {             "ClientTransactionID": f"{client_trans_id}",             "ClientID": f"{client_id}"              }               print('START:',Time.now(), cam.device.address)     start = time.time()     response = requests.request("GET","%s/%s" % (cam.device.base_url, attribute), params=pdata, headers=hdrs, verify = False)      print('consumed time:', time.time() - start, cam.device.address) # %%  unitnumlist = [1,2,3,5,6,7,8,9,10,11] camlist = [] for unitnum in unitnumlist:     #camlist.append(mainCamera(unitnum))     Thread(target = request_imagearray, kwargs = dict(cam = mainCamera(unitnum))).start() #%%  ```  and the output is ``` START: 2024-04-15 07:49:59.067946 10.0.106.6:11111 START: 2024-04-15 07:49:59.382973 10.0.106.7:11112 START: 2024-04-15 07:49:59.541495 10.0.106.8:11113 START: 2024-04-15 07:49:59.647055 10.0.106.10:11111 START: 2024-04-15 07:49:59.788433 10.0.106.11:11111 START: 2024-04-15 07:49:59.897876 10.0.106.12:11111 START: 2024-04-15 07:50:00.009254 10.0.106.13:11111 START: 2024-04-15 07:50:00.157893 10.0.106.14:11111 START: 2024-04-15 07:50:00.347704 10.0.106.16:11111 START: 2024-04-15 07:50:00.544626 10.0.106.9:11111 consumed time: 5.96204686164856 10.0.106.6:11111 consumed time: 7.304373502731323 10.0.106.7:11112 consumed time: 7.58618688583374 10.0.106.8:11113 consumed time: 7.617574453353882 10.0.106.10:11111 consumed time: 7.678117990493774 10.0.106.11:11111 consumed time: 7.76300311088562 10.0.106.12:11111 consumed time: 7.636215925216675 10.0.106.14:11111 consumed time: 7.785021066665649 10.0.106.13:11111 consumed time: 7.338518857955933 10.0.106.9:11111 consumed time: 11.309057235717773 10.0.106.16:11111 ```  The comsumed time is much longer than I expected. I expected ~3sec for all data transferred. It seems that multithreads starts to request "request.get" at the same time, but all the data is not transferred at the same time. I wrote a bot that change name of the telegram channel every N minutes But, when name of the channel is changed telegram automaticly send message, that channel name has been changed  i tried bot.delete_message command, but could't figure out how to delete that exact message I wrote a bot that change name of the telegram channel every N minutes But, when name of the channel is changed telegram automaticly send message, that channel name has been changed  i tried bot.delete_message command, but could't figure out how to delete that exact message I wrote a bot that change name of the telegram channel every N minutes But, when name of the channel is changed telegram automaticly send message, that channel name has been changed  i tried bot.delete_message command, but could't figure out how to delete that exact message To programmatically detect the message announcing a Telegram channel name change, your bot should monitor system service messages in the chat. When the channel name changes, Telegram sends a service message (with a specific message type, such as 'channel_title_changed'). Your bot can listen for this event, get the message ID of that system message, and then use bot.delete_message to remove it. Make sure your bot has proper admin permissions to delete messages in the channel.

Is any supertype and also a subtype of all types in TypeScript?  I am teaching a class on TypeScript fundamentals and I am trying to really understand the relationships between basic TS types. In all articles I saw, they put the any type very close to the top of the type hierarchy. Either  +---------+ | unknown | +---------+      |      v   +-----+   | any |   +-----+      |      v all other types Or maybe  +--------------+ | any, unknown | +--------------+      |      v all other types But I do not see it that way. You can assign value of all other types to an unknown variable but you cannot assign unknown value to anything. That puts it on top. But with any, you can assign all values of all types to any and also assign an any value to variables of all types. So it behaves as uknown and never at the same time. From this I would argue, that any stands completely aside of the type hierarchy tree not fitting anywhere in it. I would say any sidesteps the type system completely and in essence means "turn off typechecking"  Am I wrong? Is any supertype and also a subtype of all types in TypeScript? Is there a melt command in Snowflake?  Is there a Snowflake command that will transform a table like this: ``` a,b,c 1,10,0.1 2,11,0.12 3,12,0.13 ``` to a table like this: ``` key,value a,1 a,2 a,3 b,10 b,11 b,13 c,0.1 c,0.12 c,0.13 ``` ?  This operation is often called melt in other tabular systems, but the basic idea is to convert the table into a list of key value pairs. I have a custom PyTorch model that bottlenecks my application due to how it is currently used.  The application is a web server built in Flask that receives job submissions for the PyTorch model to process. Due to the processing time of each job, I use Celery to handle the computation, where Flask queues the tasks for Celery to execute.  Each job consists of loading the PyTorch model from the disk, moving the model and data to a GPU, and making a prediction on the data submitted. However, loading the model takes around 6 seconds. In many instances, that is a magnitude or two larger than prediction time.  Thus, is it possible to load the model and move it to a GPU on server startup (specifically when the Celery worker starts), avoiding the time needed to load the model and copy it to the GPU every job? Ideally, I'd want to load the model and copy it to every available GPU on server startup, leaving each Celery job to choose an available GPU and copy the data over. Currently, I only have one GPU, so a multi-GPU solution is not a requirement at the moment, but I'm planning ahead.  Further, the memory constraints of the model and data allow for only one job per GPU at a time, so I have a single Celery worker that processes jobs sequentially. This could reduce the complexity of the solution due to avoiding multiple jobs attempting to use the model in shared memory at the same time, so I figured I'd mention it.  How can I deal with it? I recently came across a code snippet (a joke) where the test() method of JavaScript's RegExp object was used with an empty object as an argument. Here's the code:  ``` console.log(new RegExp({}).test('mom')); // true console.log(new RegExp({}).test('dad')); // false ```  Can someone explain why is it happens? When you call new RegExp({}) in JavaScript, the RegExp constructor receives an empty object as its first argument. JavaScript implicitly calls the object's toString() method, which for an empty object returns '[object Object]'. So, the RegExp is created as new RegExp('[object Object]'). When you call test('mom'), it checks if the string 'mom' matches the pattern '[object Object]'. Since 'mom' does not match this pattern, it should return false. PeekMessageW and GetMessageW are both Windows API functions for retrieving messages from a thread's message queue, but they behave differently. PeekMessageW checks the message queue and, if there is a message, fills the provided MSG struct and returns immediately; if there is no message, it returns 0. GetMessageW, on the other hand, blocks until a message is available. In your case, always using PeekMessageW in a loop with Sleep is a valid way to poll for messages, but if other messages (besides WM_HOTKEY) are in the queue and not being removed

Can somebody clarify the use of the virtual modifier on interface members? I use the latest stable language version of C#. In interfaces starting from C# 8.0, you can provide default implementations for interface members. You can use the 'virtual' modifier on an interface's member with a default implementation, but its effect is limited: the 'virtual' keyword in this context means that the method can be overridden by an implementing class, but this only works with explicit interface implementation. In C#, the 'virtual' modifier in an interface does not make the member virtual in the deriving class. The implementing classes do not inherit the member as a virtual member; they must re-implement (or explicitly override) the member in

I am it to run an insert method, the insert runs fine in the stored procedure, but the return value from `ExecuteNonQuery()` always returns `-1`.  Here is my C# code:  ``` int ret = 0;    using (SqlConnection conn = new SqlConnection(this.ConnectionString))  {      using (SqlCommand cmd = new SqlCommand(QueryName, conn))      {          conn.Open();            if (Params != null)              cmd.Parameters.AddRange(Params);            cmd.CommandType = CommandType.StoredProcedure;                    ret = cmd.ExecuteNonQuery();            conn.Close();      }  }    return ret; ``` Why do I get -1 instead of the actual number of rows changed? Here is my C# code:  ``` int ret = 0;    using (SqlConnection conn = new SqlConnection(this.ConnectionString))  {      using (SqlCommand cmd = new SqlCommand(QueryName, conn))      {          conn.Open();            if (Params != null)              cmd.Parameters.AddRange(Params);            cmd.CommandType = CommandType.StoredProcedure;                    ret = cmd.ExecuteNonQuery();            conn.Close();      }  }    return ret; ``` Why do I get -1 instead of the actual number of rows changed? Why does ExecuteNonQuery() always return -1?  Ive used this method before to return the amount of rows changed. I am it to run an insert method, the insert runs fine in the stored procedure, but the return value from `ExecuteNonQuery()` always returns `-1`.  Here is my C# code:  ``` int ret = 0;    using (SqlConnection conn = new SqlConnection(this.ConnectionString))  {      using (SqlCommand cmd = new SqlCommand(QueryName, conn))      {          conn.Open();            if (Params != null)              cmd.Parameters.AddRange(Params);            cmd.CommandType = CommandType.StoredProcedure;                    ret = cmd.ExecuteNonQuery();            conn.Close();      }  }    return ret; ``` Why do I get -1 instead of the actual number of rows changed? When you use ExecuteNonQuery() with a stored procedure, it returns -1 if the stored procedure includes SET NOCOUNT ON. This setting suppresses the message about the number of rows affected. If you want ExecuteNonQuery() to return the number of rows changed, remove SET NOCOUNT ON from your stored procedure, or add SET NOCOUNT OFF. Otherwise, -1 is returned even if the insert runs successfully.

Quantization and torch_dtype in huggingface transformer  Not sure if its the right forum to ask but.    Assuming i have a gptq model that is 4bit. how does using from_pretrained(torch_dtype=torch.float16) work? In my understanding 4 bit meaning changing the weights from either 32-bit precision to 4bit precision using quantization methods.    However, calling it the torch_dtype=torch.float16 would mean the weights are in 16 bits? Am i missing something here. Model quantization (such as 4-bit GPTQ) and specifying torch_dtype in Hugging Face Transformers are related but distinct concepts. GPTQ quantization reduces the model weights to 4 bits for storage and inference efficiency. However, when you use from_pretrained(torch_dtype=torch.float16), you are instructing the Transformers library to load or cast weights as float16. If the model is already quantized to 4 bits, this setting is usually ignored and the underlying quantized format is preserved. In other words, using torch_dtype for a quantized model has no effect as quantized models handle their own

Do GTK file chooser dialogs come with localized strings for buttons and titles?  On Windows and macOS, file chooser dialogs are built into the operating system, and there is a way for an application to tell the dialog to localize the text on buttons and titles to the system language (usually by not explicitly setting the button and title text).  On Linux, while there is no system file chooser dialog, the vast majority of applications use GTK. However, GTK's file chooser dialogs require users to provide strings for the button and title text:  ``` dialog = gtk_file_chooser_dialog_new ("Open File",                                       parent_window,                                       action,                                       "_Cancel",                                       GTK_RESPONSE_CANCEL,                                       "_Open",                                       GTK_RESPONSE_ACCEPT,                                       NULL); ```  Leaving those strings empty or null leads to the buttons and title having no text at all, instead of having some default text.  Does GTK have its own set of localized default button and title text that application developers can tap into?  Note that many Linux applications use the gettext internationalization library, but that depends on the application to provide a translation database for every language they wish to support, which is not what I'm looking for. I'm looking for a way for my application to show proper dialog text in every language that GTK knows about, even if my application doesn't have translated strings for them.  Note also that that GTK message dialogs created with `gtk_message_dialog_new` can have correctly translated button text, assuming that the user has installed the correct language packs, as no text is explicitly specified (you just pass `GTK_BUTTONS_OK_CANCEL` to it to get correctly translated "OK" and "Cancel" buttons), but there seems to be no equivalent for file chooser dialogs. But when I try to deploy my services with stack to the swarm, docker engine complains that my image is not a correct reposity/tag, with the exact following message :  ``` Error response from daemon: rpc error: code = 3 desc = ContainerSpec: "GROUP/IMAGE:" is not a valid repository/tag ```  To fix this, I can fix the image version directly in the docker-compose.yml file. Is there any logic here or it that a bug? But this mixes fix part of the docker-compose and variable ones.  Cheers, Olivier Docker Compose can use environment variables in the image field of your docker-compose.yml file, such as image: myrepo/myimage:${IMAGE_VERSION}. However, when using 'docker stack deploy', Docker Swarm expects all variable substitutions to be performed before deploying, as it does not substitute variables from the .env file at deploy time the way 'docker-compose' does. You need to ensure that you run 'docker-compose config' to render the file with substituted variables before using it with 'docker stack deploy'. Alternatively, you can use a tool or script to substitute the variables in your compose file as a build

Notice in Python version is 3.9.18 and 3.11.4  The snippet of code above, I'd expected a() returns 100 Assigning a new function to an instance's `__call__` attribute using `setattr(a, '__call__', new_func)` does not change the behavior of calling the instance with `a()`. In Python, special methods like `__call__` are only looked up on the class, not the instance, when used with their syntactic sugar (i.e., `a()`). When you trigger a workflow using workflow_dispatch in GitHub Actions, the workflow file executed is always the one present in the branch that you select in the 'Run workflow' dropdown. This means you can create or modify workflows on any branch (not just 'master' or 'main'), and then execute those branch-specific versions by selecting the appropriate branch before running the workflow. This behavior allows you to test workflow changes or experiment with new actions in feature branches before merging them.

I've thought about two options: adding extra fields to the model (one field for language) or creating another model with all texts in every language. On the other hand, I've seen that there are some libraries such as django-modeltranslation that are intended to solve that issue, however, I'd like to know some opinions about them. What do you think? What would you recommend to me?  Thank you very much To configure Django Rest Framework (DRF) serializers to automatically return translated fields based on the user's language preference, the best approach is to use a package like django-modeltranslation or django-parler. These libraries let you define translatable fields in your models and handle fetching the right language transparently. For serializers, you usually don't have to do anything special—django-modeltranslation, for instance, will serve the correct language fields based on the user/request's active language (using Django's translation machinery). In summary, using a dedicated translation package is recommended for maintainability and scalability over maintaining extra fields manually. This

(for example if numpy version is specified as 1.1 but previous package in the list automatically installs numpy of last version!) (for example if numpy version is specified as 1.1 but previous package in the list automatically installs numpy of last version!)  I installed librosa==0.9.2 which installed scipy 1.10 during its installation. but after that there is scipy=1.8 in the list. doesn't it make a conflict? When pip installs packages from a requirements.txt file, it processes the entire file and attempts to resolve all dependencies and their version constraints. If different packages require conflicting versions of the same dependency (for example, librosa requires scipy 1.10 but another line specifies scipy==1.8), pip will try to find a compatible version. If no compatible version exists that satisfies all requirements, pip will return a version conflict error and stop installation. Specifying incompatible versions in requirements.txt can cause installation to fail unless all dependencies can be resolved. To avoid these issues, it's important to synchronize the versions in your requirements

To load nested structs with complex join conditions in GORM without relying on Preload, you must use explicit SQL joins with GORM’s Joins and Select methods. Preload issues separate queries for associations and ignores your custom join conditions. How do I compare strings in Java?  I've been using the `==` operator in my program to compare all my strings so far. However, I ran into a bug, changed one of them into `.equals()` instead, and it fixed the bug.  Is `==` bad? When should it and should it not be used? What's the difference? How do I compare strings in Java?  I've been using the `==` operator in my program to compare all my strings so far. However, I ran into a bug, changed one of them into `.equals()` instead, and it fixed the bug.  Is `==` bad? When should it and should it not be used? What's the difference? In Java, `==` checks whether two references point to the same object (reference equality), while `.equals()` checks whether the two objects have the same value (content equality). For strings, you should use `.equals()` when you want to compare the contents of two strings. Using `==` with strings can cause subtle bugs because it only returns true if both references point to the exact same object in memory. In general, use `==` only if you want to check if two references are identical, but for comparing string values, always use `.equals()`.
